column6: How many years had investments of over 22 Billion Euros in the years 2011 to 2014?
column7: Restrict attention to the bars for years 2011 through 2014. Check which of those bars have an investments value greater than 22 on the y-axis. Count the qualifying years to get 3.

column6: in which years does south korea have a bigger value than france?
column7: Compare the South Korea and France bar segments within each Period. Mark each Period where the South Korea segment is taller than the France segment. List those Period labels as 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, and Jan-Oct 2019.

column6: What is the year with the largest increase compared to the previous year?
column7: Calculate the increase in “In millions” between each adjacent pair of years shown on the line. Compare these year-to-year increases to find the largest one. Select the later year of the pair with the largest increase, which is 2010.

column6: What is the average of Season's ticket prices with a ticket price of $60 or less?
column7: Filter the bars to the seasons whose “Average ticket price in US dollars” is at or below 60. Sum those filtered ticket prices to get 320.43. Divide that sum by the count of filtered seasons (6) to get an average of 53.99.
column8: {"ops": [{"op": "filter", "id": "n1", "meta": {"nodeId": "n1", "inputs": [], "sentenceIndex": 1}, "field": "Average ticket price in US dollars", "operator": "<=", "value": 60}, {"op": "average", "id": "n2", "meta": {"nodeId": "n2", "inputs": ["n1"], "sentenceIndex": 1}, "field": "Average ticket price in US dollars"}]}

column6: What is the difference between the second largest and the smallest values between 2000 and 2008?
column7: Filter the chart to the years 2000 through 2008 inclusive. Sort those values by Percentage_of_Population to find the smallest value and the second-largest value. Subtract the smallest value from the second-largest value, leaving a difference of 0.019.

column6: What is the difference between the largest value in Germany and the smallest value in the U.S. between 2010 and 2015?
column7: Filter the chart to the years 2010 through 2015 inclusive. Find the maximum Favorable_View_Percentage among Germany’s points in that range. Find the minimum Favorable_View_Percentage among the U.S. points in that range. Subtract the U.S. minimum from the Germany maximum to get 31.

column6: What is the difference between the average and the maximum number?
column7: Find the maximum bar height in the chart to get 380. Calculate the average of the “Number of fires” values across all years to get 230. Subtract the average from the maximum, leaving a difference of 150.

column6: How many diet types surpassed the threshold of 0.03% in 2019?
column7: Focus on the bars for the year 2019 across all diet-type panels. Compare each 2019 bar’s height to the threshold value of 0.03 on the y-axis. Count the diet types whose 2019 share is strictly greater than 0.03. The total number that surpasses the threshold is 9.

column6: What is the difference between Scotland's largest value and England and Wales's smallest value?
column7: Find Scotland’s highest SharePercentage across the response-category panels to get 28. Check England & Wales’s lowest SharePercentage across the same panels to get 2. Subtract the smaller value from the larger to get 26.

column6: What is the average value of top 3 malls' per sqaure foot cost?
column7: Sort the bars by US dollars per square foot in descending order. Select the top three malls’ values from the chart. Add those three values and divide by 3 to get an average of 600.

column6: In average and median values, which one is bigger?
column7: Calculate the average of the Number_of_Fatalities values across all years to get about 167.6. Sort the Number_of_Fatalities values and take the middle (11th) value to get a median of 173. Compare the two summary values to see that the average is bigger.
column9: exec: avg>median = False → median is bigger

column6: How many years has Russia had higher favorability than the US?
column7: Compare the Russia and US favorable-view percentages for each year shown on the chart. Count the years where the Russia value is greater than the US value. The total number of such years is 3.
column8: {"ops": [{"op": "pairDiff", "id": "n1", "meta": {"nodeId": "n1", "inputs": [], "sentenceIndex": 1}, "by": "Year", "groupA": "Russia favorability in US", "groupB": "US favorability in Russia", "seriesField": "Favorability_Direction", "field": "Favorable_View_Percentage", "signed": true}], "ops2": [{"op": "filter", "id": "n2", "meta": {"nodeId": "n2", "inputs": ["n1"], "sentenceIndex": 2}, "operator": ">", "value": 0}], "ops3": [{"op": "count", "id": "n3", "meta": {"nodeId": "n3", "inputs": ["n2"], "sentenceIndex": 3}}]}

column6: What is the sum of the differences between women and men in the last five years?
column7: Treat the last five years as 2016 through 2020. Subtract the men’s value from the women’s value for each of those years to get the yearly gender differences. Sum those five yearly differences to get -172.26.
column9: exec: -163.28 [fix: Year>2015 replaces between (dropped final year on 2-row/yr data)]

column6: Which city had the biggest jump in population from 2010 to 2025?
column7: Compute the population change for each city by subtracting the 2010 bar height from the 2025 bar height. Compare these changes across all city panels. Select the city with the largest increase, which is Delhi.

column6: What is the net income sum from 2010 to 2015?
column7: Select the bars for the years 2010 through 2015 inclusive. Add their “Net income in USD” values together. The resulting total net income is 41581.

column6: What is the average percentage of non-black races that they answered very interested?
column7: Treat “non-black races” as White, Hispanic, and Other in the chart. Get the Share_of_Respondents values for the “Very interested” segment for those three races. Average those three values to obtain 19%.

column6: How many years correspond to a value between 20 and 30?
column7: Filter the chart to the years whose FIFA World Ranking position lies between 20 and 30 inclusive. Count the remaining year points to get 10.
column8: {"ops": [{"op": "filter", "id": "n1", "meta": {"nodeId": "n1", "inputs": [], "sentenceIndex": 1}, "field": "FIFA World Ranking position", "operator": ">=", "value": 20}, {"op": "filter", "id": "n2", "meta": {"nodeId": "n2", "inputs": ["n1"], "sentenceIndex": 1}, "field": "FIFA World Ranking position", "operator": "<=", "value": 30}], "ops2": [{"op": "count", "id": "n3", "meta": {"nodeId": "n3", "inputs": ["n2"], "sentenceIndex": 2}}]}

column6: What is the sum of number of employees from Europe region before 2015?
column7: Treat the Europe region as the bars colored Germany, Great Britain, Spain, and Rest of Europe. Filter the chart to the years before 2015 (2011–2014). Sum the Number_of_Employees values across those regions and years to get 166816.

column6: What is the Year that has the biggest deviation from the total average?
column7: Group the chart’s points by year using the year portion of the Month Year labels. Calculate the overall average Consumer Price Index across all plotted months. Compute each year’s average Consumer Price Index from its months. Compare the absolute difference between each year’s average and the overall average. Select the year with the largest deviation, which is 2020.
column9: exec: max |dev|=7.64 at May '21 (→2021); per-point diffByValue (per-year averaging not in grammar)

column6: What is the sum of the number of weights exceeding 3670 in the Boys group and the number of weights exceeding 3550 in the Girls group?
column7: Filter the chart to the Boys group and count the years where the average weight is greater than 3670. Filter the chart to the Girls group and count the years where the average weight is greater than 3550. Add the two counts together to get 5.

column6: Which year shows the lowest gap between convenience and price
column7: Select the Convenience and Price values for each year from the chart. Calculate the absolute difference between those two values within each year. Compare these yearly gaps to find the smallest one. The year with the lowest gap is 2013.
column8: {"ops": [{"op": "pairDiff", "id": "n1", "meta": {"nodeId": "n1", "inputs": [], "sentenceIndex": 1}, "by": "Year", "groupA": "Convenience", "groupB": "Price", "seriesField": "Metric", "field": "Has a great impact", "absolute": true}], "ops2": [{"op": "findExtremum", "id": "n2", "meta": {"nodeId": "n2", "inputs": ["n1"], "sentenceIndex": 2}, "which": "min", "field": "Has a great impact"}]}

column6: What is the difference in the average between North America and Latin America?
column7: Collect the North America values across all years and average them to get 9.578. Collect the Latin America values across all years and average them to get 1.156. Subtract the Latin America average from the North America average, leaving a difference of 8.62.

column7: Filter the chart to the Agriculture-colored segment for each year. Compare the heights (Share_of_GDP values) of those Agriculture segments across all years. Select the year whose Agriculture segment is tallest

column6: Which method shows the highest difference between every day and less often?
column7: Locate the “EVERY DAY” and “LESS OFTEN” segments within each method’s stacked bar. Calculate the absolute difference between those two segment values for each method. Compare these differences across methods. Select the method with the largest gap
